stream.definition: To send or receive audio, video, or data continuously over the internet in real time, without downloading the entire file first; also, a steady flow of water, information, or events.
stream.example: Fans across the country stream the concert live on their phones.
stream.usageNote: Digital streaming took off with broadband; context tells you whether it means a creek or a live broadcast.
stream.etymology: From Old English strēam (flowing water); the media sense emerged in the 1990s–2000s.

podcast.definition: A digital audio series released in episodes that listeners can download or stream on demand.
podcast.example: She subscribed to a science podcast that drops every Tuesday morning.
podcast.usageNote: Coined from iPod + broadcast; most podcasts are free with optional paid tiers.
podcast.etymology: Blend of iPod and broadcast, popularized around 2004.

emoji.definition: A small digital icon or picture used in messages and social posts to express an idea, emotion, or reaction.
emoji.example: He added a thumbs-up emoji to show he agreed with the plan.
emoji.usageNote: Tone can vary by platform and culture — the same emoji may read differently on different devices.

hashtag.definition: A word or phrase preceded by # that groups public posts on social media around a topic or event.
hashtag.example: Students used #ScienceFair2026 to share project photos.
hashtag.usageNote: Hashtags are searchable metadata, not part of the sentence grammar.

sandbox.definition: An isolated testing environment where code or software can run safely without affecting production systems or real user data.
sandbox.example: Developers prototype new features in a sandbox before shipping them live.
sandbox.usageNote: Common in software security and game design (open-world sandboxes).

middleware.definition: Software that sits between applications and databases or services, handling routing, authentication, messaging, or other shared plumbing.
middleware.example: Middleware translated the mobile app's requests into the legacy billing system.
middleware.usageNote: You rarely notice middleware until it fails — then everything downstream breaks.

devops.definition: A practice combining software development and IT operations to ship reliable software faster through automation, monitoring, and collaboration.
devops.example: Their DevOps pipeline runs tests automatically on every code commit.
devops.usageNote: DevOps is a culture and toolchain, not a single job title or product.

ci/cd.definition: Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery — automated pipelines that build, test, and deploy code changes frequently and predictably.
ci/cd.example: CI/CD caught the failing unit test before the bug reached production.
ci/cd.usageNote: CI merges and tests often; CD extends that to staging or production releases.

bookmark.definition: A saved link or marker that lets you return quickly to a webpage, document, or passage later.
bookmark.example: She bookmarked the dictionary entry to review before the quiz.
bookmark.usageNote: Browser bookmarks differ from social-media bookmarks, but the idea is the same.

cloud.definition: Remote servers accessed over the internet where data and applications are stored and run instead of on a local device.
cloud.example: Photos sync to the cloud so they survive a lost phone.
cloud.usageNote: The cloud is physical data centers — not literally the sky.

algorithm.definition: A step-by-step procedure for solving a problem or completing a task, especially one encoded for a computer.
algorithm.example: The recommendation algorithm surfaced words she had marked as hard.
algorithm.usageNote: Algorithms shape feeds, maps, and search — understanding inputs helps you interpret outputs.

bandwidth.definition: The capacity of a network connection to carry data in a given time, or metaphorically, personal time and energy available for tasks.
bandwidth.example: 4K streaming needs more bandwidth than a voice call.
bandwidth.usageNote: Informally, I don't have the bandwidth means I'm too busy.

cache.definition: A temporary store of data kept close to where it is needed so future requests load faster.
cache.example: The app cache made yesterday's word list appear instantly offline.
cache.usageNote: Caches can show stale content until they refresh.

debug.definition: To find and fix errors in code or a system, often using logs, breakpoints, or test cases.
debug.example: They debugged the crash by reproducing the steps on a test device.
debug.usageNote: Good debug notes save hours the next time the bug appears.

firewall.definition: A security system that monitors and filters network traffic according to rules, blocking unauthorized access.
firewall.example: The school firewall blocked downloads from unknown app stores.
firewall.usageNote: Firewalls can be hardware appliances or software on your laptop.

metadata.definition: Data that describes other data — such as author, date, file size, or tags — making content easier to find and organize.
metadata.example: Photo metadata recorded when and where the picture was taken.
metadata.usageNote: Privacy settings may strip location metadata before you share an image.

open source.definition: Software whose source code is publicly available to use, study, modify, and distribute under an approved license.
open source.example: The team chose an open-source dictionary tool they could customize legally.
open source.usageNote: Open source still has licenses — read them before commercial use.

phishing.definition: A fraudulent attempt to trick someone into revealing passwords or financial details, often through fake emails or websites.
phishing.example: The phishing message mimicked the bank's logo but used a suspicious link.
phishing.usageNote: Verify senders out-of-band; never click urgent password links blindly.

router.definition: A device that forwards data packets between networks, commonly connecting home devices to the internet.
router.example: Restarting the router restored Wi‑Fi in the classroom.
router.usageNote: Router and modem roles differ though one box may combine both.

server.definition: A computer or program that provides data, files, or services to other computers called clients.
server.example: The quiz scores sync to a server when you're back online.
server.usageNote: Cloud servers scale up during traffic spikes.

sync.definition: To keep data consistent across multiple devices or users by updating copies whenever changes occur.
sync.example: Favorites sync between her phone and tablet overnight.
sync.usageNote: Sync conflicts happen when two devices edit the same note offline.

upload.definition: To transfer a file from a local device to a remote server or cloud storage.
upload.example: Upload your sentence notes before switching phones.
upload.usageNote: Upload speed is often slower than download on home connections.

username.definition: A unique name that identifies an account on a website or service, often paired with a password.
username.example: Pick a username that doesn't reveal your full real name.
username.usageNote: Usernames may be public even when passwords stay secret.

wi-fi.definition: A wireless networking technology that connects devices to the internet or each other using radio waves.
wi-fi.example: Guest Wi‑Fi let visitors download the app without using mobile data.
wi-fi.usageNote: Wi‑Fi security (WPA3) matters on public networks.

blockchain.definition: A distributed digital ledger where records are linked in blocks and secured by cryptography across many computers.
blockchain.example: The article explained how blockchain creates tamper-evident transaction history.
blockchain.usageNote: Not every product labeled blockchain needs a blockchain.

machine learning.definition: A branch of AI where systems improve at tasks by finding patterns in data rather than following only hand-written rules.
machine learning.example: Machine learning models can rank review words you struggle with most.
machine learning.usageNote: Models reflect biases present in training data.

encryption.definition: The process of encoding information so only authorized parties with the correct key can read it.
encryption.example: End-to-end encryption kept the message unreadable on the server.
encryption.usageNote: Encryption protects data in transit and at rest when implemented correctly.

biomechanics.definition: The study of forces, motion, and structure in living bodies, especially how muscles and joints produce athletic movement.
biomechanics.example: Coaches used biomechanics to refine her sprint start technique.
biomechanics.usageNote: Biomechanics links physics with anatomy and training.

doping.definition: The use of banned substances or methods to unfairly enhance athletic performance.
doping.example: Anti-doping tests aim to keep competition fair for all athletes.
doping.usageNote: Rules vary by sport and governing body.
